events.title: Chicago First-Saturday Board Game Night
events.description: Casual meetup focused on light-to-medium modern board games. New players welcome; rules will be taught. Feel free to bring your favorite 2–4 player games.

events.title: Co-op and Campaign Board Games in Chicago
events.description: An evening dedicated to cooperative and campaign-style games like Gloomhaven, Spirit Island, and Pandemic. We’ll organize tables by complexity and experience level.

events.title: Weeknight Strategy Board Game Meetup - Chicago Loop
events.description: Mid-week meetup for medium and heavy strategy games. Great for players who already know the basics and want to dive into longer titles.

events.title: Spring Family Board Game Afternoon
events.description: Family-friendly board game afternoon with classic and modern titles suitable for kids 8+ and adults. Short teach-and-play sessions throughout the day.

events.title: Milwaukee Monday Board Gamers Meetup
events.description: Weekly open board gaming night in downtown Milwaukee. All experience levels welcome; bring games or join existing tables.

events.title: Seattle Waterfront Golden Hour Photo Walk
events.description: Join local photographers for a golden hour walk along the waterfront and Pike Place Market. We’ll focus on street and cityscape shots, followed by a casual critique.

events.title: Night Photography and Long Exposure Workshop
events.description: Hands-on workshop covering city night photography, light trails, and long exposures. Tripod recommended. We’ll start with a brief classroom session, then head outside.

events.title: Beginner Vegetable Gardening Workshop
events.description: Learn the basics of starting a vegetable garden in raised beds and containers. Topics include soil preparation, seed starting, and watering schedules.

events.title: Online Knitting Clinic: Fixing Dropped Stitches
events.description: A live video session focused on diagnosing and fixing dropped stitches in both knit and purl fabric, plus preventative tips for beginners.

events.title: NYC 40-Mile Social Cycling Loop
events.description: Social-paced ride across bridges and along bike paths in NYC. Helmets required; bring flat repair kit and water. Not a race—no-drop pace.

events.title: Digital Art Jam: Character Design Sprint
events.description: A two-hour online jam session focused on rapid character concepting. Prompts will be provided, and participants can share screens for feedback.

events.title: DIY & Crafts Swap and Show-and-Tell
events.description: Bring your finished projects and unused supplies to swap with other crafters. Short show-and-tell slots available for anyone who wants to present a project.

discussions.title: Looking for 2-player cooperative board game recommendations
discussions.body:
My partner and I have been really enjoying cooperative games lately and are looking for more 2-player friendly options. We already own Pandemic and Spirit Island and love both, but we’d like a mix of lighter weeknight games and deeper titles for weekends.

What are your favorite 2-player cooperative games that play well at that count (even if the box says 1–4 players)? Any must-try campaigns or shorter scenarios you’d recommend, and why? Please share what you like about them and roughly how long they take to play.

discussions.title: Need 5 quick vegetarian dinners under 30 minutes
discussions.body:
I’m trying to build a small rotation of go-to vegetarian dinners that I can cook on busy weeknights. Ideally I’d love at least 5 recipes that:

- Are fully vegetarian (eggs and dairy are fine)
- Take 30 minutes or less from start to finish
- Do NOT require an oven (stovetop, Instant Pot, or microwave are okay)

If you have any favorite pastas, stir-fries, skillet meals, or hearty salads that fit these criteria, please share the recipe or a rough outline of how you make them. Tips for prepping components ahead of time for faster weeknights are also very welcome!

discussions.title: City night photography tips without a high-end camera?
discussions.body:
I’ve been getting into night photography around my city—street scenes, light trails, and reflections after it rains—but I’m working with a mid-range mirrorless and a basic kit lens. I’m struggling with noisy images and blurry shots when people are moving.

For those who shoot city night scenes regularly, what settings, techniques, or simple gear upgrades made the biggest difference for you? I’m especially interested in how you balance shutter speed, aperture, and ISO in real-world situations, and any tricks you use for focusing in low light.

discussions.title: Help! Dropped stitch several rows back in my first scarf
discussions.body:
I’m knitting my very first garter-stitch scarf and just realized I dropped a stitch several rows back. The loose loop is hanging there and I’m scared to touch it in case I make things worse. The yarn is a medium-weight wool blend on size 8 needles.

How do I safely fix a dropped stitch that’s more than one row down without ripping out the whole project? Clear explanations or step-by-step photos/videos would be amazing, because I’m still very new to reading my stitches.

discussions.title: Renter-friendly living room makeover ideas (no painting allowed)
discussions.body:
I’m renting a small apartment and the living room is super bland: beige walls, beige carpet, and I’m not allowed to paint or mount anything heavy. I’d love to make it feel cozy and stylish without risking my security deposit.

What renter-friendly ideas do you have for transforming a very plain living room—things like removable solutions, textiles, lighting, or layout tricks? Bonus points for examples or photos of what you’ve done in your own rentals.

discussions.title: Beginner balcony gardening: soil and pot advice for veggies?
discussions.body:
I’m a complete beginner trying to grow a few vegetables on my small apartment balcony this year (probably herbs, cherry tomatoes, and maybe lettuce). I’m confused about what kind of soil mix and pot size I actually need.

If you’ve done container gardening on a balcony, what type of potting soil or soil mix worked best for you, and how big should the containers be for tomatoes and lettuce? Any beginner-friendly tips for drainage, watering, and avoiding root problems would really help!

discussions.title: DIY pallet vertical garden wall: step-by-step project log
discussions.body:
I finally finished my pallet-based vertical garden wall for the patio and documented the whole build. It uses two reclaimed pallets, landscape fabric, and a mix of herbs and flowers. I ran into a few surprises with weight, drainage, and mounting it safely.

I’ve posted my step-by-step process, materials list, and photos throughout the build. I’d love feedback on how to improve the design for better drainage and plant health, and I’m happy to answer questions if anyone wants to try a similar project.

discussions.title: February character design sprint: 7-day prompt challenge
discussions.body:
Let’s do a one-week character design sprint together! For the next 7 days, we’ll have a daily prompt (posted in this thread) and everyone can share their sketches or finished pieces. All skill levels and styles are welcome—traditional or digital.

Post your work-in-progress, ask for feedback, or just cheer others on. If you miss a day, no worries; you can always catch up later. I’ll gather the prompts and a small gallery of favorites at the end of the week.

discussions.title: Soil health basics for new gardeners
discussions.body:
I see a lot of beginner questions about fertilizer and plant problems that actually come back to soil health. I thought it might be helpful to start a thread that covers the basics: structure, organic matter, drainage, and simple amendments.

If you’re new to gardening, what confuses you most about soil? And for more experienced folks, what are your favorite low-cost ways to improve soil quality over a few seasons (compost, cover crops, mulching, etc.)?

discussions.title: Chicago board game meetups for May 2025
discussions.body:
I’m trying to plan out which board game meetups to attend in Chicago this May and noticed a bunch of events popping up on the calendar. There are family afternoons, co-op nights, and heavier strategy meetups all over the city.

If you’ve attended any of the local events at Dice & Meeples Cafe, Lakeview Community Center, or the Loop Hobby Club, which ones would you recommend for someone new to the Chicago scene? I’d love to hear what the vibe is like and which events are best for meeting people.

discussions.title: Tutorial: fixing a dropped stitch in garter and stockinette
discussions.body:
I’ve answered a lot of questions about dropped stitches recently, so I put together a simple tutorial. This post covers how to identify the ladder of yarn, pick up a dropped stitch with a crochet hook, and get it back on the needle in both garter and stockinette.

I’ve included notes on common mistakes (twisted stitches, uneven tension) and when it’s easier to carefully rip back instead. If you’re stuck, feel free to post clear photos of your project and I’ll try to walk you through it.

discussions.title: Commuter bike setup check: what would you change?
discussions.body:
I recently converted an older road bike into my daily commuter and I’m not sure if I’ve optimized the setup. I’m riding about 8 miles each way in city traffic with hills, rain, and occasional potholes.

What do you consider must-haves for a reliable commuter (fenders, tires, lights, bags, gearing, etc.)? If you’re willing, I’d love to hear how you’ve set up your own commuter and what upgrades made the biggest difference in comfort and safety.

discussions.title: Favorite oven-free vegetarian meals for hot summer nights?
discussions.body:
When it’s really hot out I hate turning on the oven, but I still want something more satisfying than just salad. I’m looking for vegetarian meals I can make on the stovetop, in an Instant Pot, or completely no-cook.

What are your go-to oven-free dinners for summer—especially ones that don’t feel too heavy in the heat? Bonus points if they’re relatively quick and use seasonal produce.

posts.content:
My partner and I have been really enjoying cooperative games lately and are looking for more 2-player friendly options. We already own Pandemic and Spirit Island and love both, but we’d like a mix of lighter weeknight games and deeper titles for weekends.

What are your favorite 2-player cooperative games that play well at that count (even if the box says 1–4 players)? Any must-try campaigns or shorter scenarios you’d recommend, and why? Please share what you like about them and roughly how long they take to play.

For lighter weeknights, I highly recommend Codenames: Duet and The Crew. Both are super easy to teach, scale perfectly to 2 players, and play in about 20–30 minutes. They also have built-in campaigns if you want a bit of progression without a huge rules overhead.

If you like Spirit Island, you might enjoy Aeon’s End as a 2-player co-op. It has that same puzzle-y feel but with deckbuilding, and the turn order deck keeps things tense. Games run about 45–60 minutes once you know the rules, and there’s a lot of replayability with different bosses.
Why are people still posting these basic questions? Just google it instead of wasting everyone’s time here.

I’m trying to build a small rotation of go-to vegetarian dinners that I can cook on busy weeknights. Ideally I’d love at least 5 recipes that:

- Are fully vegetarian (eggs and dairy are fine)
- Take 30 minutes or less from start to finish
- Do NOT require an oven (stovetop, Instant Pot, or microwave are okay)

If you have any favorite pastas, stir-fries, skillet meals, or hearty salads that fit these criteria, please share the recipe or a rough outline of how you make them. Tips for prepping components ahead of time for faster weeknights are also very welcome!

One of my go-tos is a 20-minute chickpea and spinach curry. Sauté onion, garlic, and ginger, add curry powder and tomato paste, then stir in canned chickpeas and coconut milk and simmer while you make rice. Another super fast option is a veggie quesadilla: pre-sauté peppers, onions, and black beans on Sunday and you can assemble and pan-fry quesadillas in under 10 minutes on weeknights.

I’m knitting my very first garter-stitch scarf and just realized I dropped a stitch several rows back. The loose loop is hanging there and I’m scared to touch it in case I make things worse. The yarn is a medium-weight wool blend on size 8 needles.

How do I safely fix a dropped stitch that’s more than one row down without ripping out the whole project? Clear explanations or step-by-step photos/videos would be amazing, because I’m still very new to reading my stitches.

You can absolutely fix this without ripping everything out. For fixing a dropped stitch in garter stitch, grab a small crochet hook and locate the loose loop plus the horizontal strands (the “ladder”) above it. Insert the hook through the dropped loop from front to back, then pull the lowest ladder strand through to form a new stitch; repeat for each row until you reach the current row, alternating whether you bring the strand through from the front or back to match your knit/garter texture.

Once you’re done fixing a dropped stitch, place the newly rescued stitch back on your left needle, making sure the right leg of the stitch is in front so it isn’t twisted. The fabric might look a little uneven for a few rows, but a gentle tug and blocking at the end usually even everything out.

I know this is off-topic, but the real problem is how little funding crafts and home-ec programs get compared to everything else, and it all comes back to national politics. That’s a much bigger issue than a single dropped stitch in a scarf.

I’m renting a small apartment and the living room is super bland: beige walls, beige carpet, and I’m not allowed to paint or mount anything heavy. I’d love to make it feel cozy and stylish without risking my security deposit.

What renter-friendly ideas do you have for transforming a very plain living room—things like removable solutions, textiles, lighting, or layout tricks? Bonus points for examples or photos of what you’ve done in your own rentals.

Big area rugs and curtain panels will do a lot of work for you here. Layer a colorful rug over the beige carpet, use tension rods for floor-to-ceiling curtains, and add peel-and-stick decals or removable washi tape “frames” for prints hung with command strips. Swapping in warm-toned bulbs and a couple of floor lamps can also change the whole mood without touching the walls.

If you really want a stylish space, you should hire our premium online interior design service. We’ll create a full 3D layout and shopping list for you, and you can get 20% off with code DECORBOOST at our partner store. Check my profile for the link and book a package today.

I’m a complete beginner trying to grow a few vegetables on my small apartment balcony this year (probably herbs, cherry tomatoes, and maybe lettuce). I’m confused about what kind of soil mix and pot size I actually need.

If you’ve done container gardening on a balcony, what type of potting soil or soil mix worked best for you, and how big should the containers be for tomatoes and lettuce? Any beginner-friendly tips for drainage, watering, and avoiding root problems would really help!

For balcony containers, use a high-quality potting mix labeled for containers rather than garden soil—look for one with peat or coco coir, perlite, and some compost. For cherry tomatoes, aim for at least a 5-gallon pot with good drainage holes; for lettuce and herbs, 6–8 inch deep planters are usually fine. Elevate the pots slightly on feet or bricks so excess water can drain freely and you don’t end up with soggy roots.

I do balcony veggies too and second the container potting mix suggestion. I also mix in a bit of compost every season and top the soil with mulch (shredded leaves or straw) to keep it from drying out too fast on hot days. Just make sure the soil level sits a couple of inches below the rim so water doesn’t spill over the sides before it has a chance to soak in.

This is super helpful, thank you both. One follow-up question about soil: if I can only buy one bag, is it better to get a basic container potting mix and add compost later, or should I look for something labeled as a vegetable-specific soil mix from the start?

Also, how can I tell when the soil in a balcony container is staying too wet versus drying out too quickly? I’m worried about accidentally overwatering and causing root rot.

I finally finished my pallet-based vertical garden wall for the patio and documented the whole build. It uses two reclaimed pallets, landscape fabric, and a mix of herbs and flowers. I ran into a few surprises with weight, drainage, and mounting it safely.

Here’s my step-by-step process, materials list, and build notes. I’d love feedback on how to improve the design for better drainage and plant health, and I’m happy to answer questions if anyone wants to try a similar project.

I see a lot of beginner questions about fertilizer and plant problems that actually come back to soil health. I thought it might be helpful to start a thread that covers the basics: structure, organic matter, drainage, and simple amendments.

If you’re new to gardening, what confuses you most about soil? And for more experienced folks, what are your favorite low-cost ways to improve soil quality over a few seasons (compost, cover crops, mulching, etc.)?
